skill.name: Apple HIG: Layout and Navigation Components
skill.description: Apple Human Interface Guidelines for layout and navigation components including sidebars, split views, tab bars, scroll views, windows, panels, lists, tables, and outline views.
skill.summary: Design adaptive Apple app layouts using official HIG patterns for sidebars, split views, tab bars, and navigation hierarchies across iOS, iPadOS, macOS, and visionOS.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill contains only documentation markdown files referencing Apple Human Interface Guidelines. All 768 static analysis findings are false positives: backtick patterns are markdown code blocks with API names, URLs link to official Apple Developer documentation, and cryptographic/C2 keywords are spurious text matches in design documentation. No executable code, network calls, or system commands present.

content.user_title: Design adaptive Apple app layouts with HIG navigation patterns
content.value_statement: Building apps for multiple Apple platforms requires understanding complex navigation patterns and layout adaptation. This skill provides official Apple Human Interface Guidelines for sidebars, split views, tab bars, and more to ensure your app follows platform conventions.
actual_capabilities: Provide official Apple HIG guidance for sidebar, split view, tab bar, and scroll view components
Recommend navigation patterns based on app structure and target platforms
Explain layout adaptation across iPhone, iPad, Mac, and visionOS size classes
Reference SwiftUI and UIKit API documentation for navigation components
Guide multitasking support for iPad Split View and Stage Manager
Clarify platform-specific conventions for lists, tables, outline views, and windows
limitations: Provides documentation references only - does not generate executable code
Focused on Apple platform conventions - not applicable to Android or web design
Does not replace official Apple Developer Documentation for implementation details
Requires user to have Xcode and appropriate Apple SDKs for actual development

use_cases.title: iOS App Navigation Design
use_cases.description: A developer building their first iPad app needs to decide between tab bars and sidebars for navigation. The skill explains when to use each pattern and how NavigationSplitView adapts across size classes.
use_cases.target_user: iOS developer new to iPad development

use_cases.title: Cross-Platform Layout Strategy
use_cases.description: A team launching on iPhone, iPad, and Mac needs a unified navigation strategy. The skill provides the adaptation checklist and pattern selection table to plan responsive layouts.

use_cases.title: visionOS Spatial Layout
use_cases.description: A developer creating their first visionOS app needs to understand windows, volumes, and ornaments. The skill explains spatial positioning and ornament attachment patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Basic navigation pattern selection
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm building an app with 4 main sections: Home, Search, Library, and Settings. Which navigation pattern should I use for iPhone vs iPad?

prompt_templates.title: Adaptive layout guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: My app uses a three-column layout on iPad but breaks on iPhone. How should I adapt this using NavigationSplitView?

prompt_templates.title: Platform-specific conventions
prompt_templates.prompt: What are the differences between macOS sidebars and iPad sidebars? When should I use each pattern?

prompt_templates.title: Advanced multitasking support
prompt_templates.prompt: My iPad app needs to support Split View, Slide Over, and Stage Manager. What size class transitions should I test and how do I handle them?

output_examples.input: I'm building a document editor with an inspector panel. Should I use a sidebar or panel?
output_examples.output: For a document editor with an inspector, use a panel (not a sidebar) on macOS. Sidebars are for primary navigation between sections, while panels are for secondary controls and properties that supplement the main content. On iPad, present the inspector as a popover or sheet. Reference: panels.md for inspector patterns, sidebars.md for primary navigation patterns.

output_examples.input: How do I handle tab bars when I have 6 top-level sections?
output_examples.output: iOS tab bars support a maximum of 5 visible tabs. With 6 sections, use a 'More' tab for additional sections. However, consider whether 6 top-level sections indicates an opportunity to restructure with a sidebar (iPad) or nested navigation. For iPadOS 18+, use .sidebarAdaptable to automatically show sidebar on iPad and tab bar on iPhone. Reference: tab-bars.md for tab count guidance, sidebars.md for alternative patterns.

best_practices: Always check for existing apple-design-context.md before asking questions - use information already available
Match navigation pattern to information architecture depth: shallow structures use tab bars, deep hierarchies use sidebars
Test layout adaptation at every size class transition and iPad multitasking ratio
anti_patterns: Using tab bars on iPad when sidebar would better handle deep hierarchies
Building fixed-width layouts that do not adapt to compact vs regular size classes
Ignoring visionOS spatial conventions when targeting Apple Vision Pro

faq.question: When should I use a sidebar vs a tab bar?
faq.answer: Use tab bars for 3-5 peer top-level sections with flat navigation (iPhone). Use sidebars for deep hierarchical navigation or when you have many sections (iPad, Mac). iPadOS 18+ supports .sidebarAdaptable which automatically shows sidebar on iPad and tab bar on iPhone.

faq.question: How do I make my layout work on both iPhone and iPad?
faq.answer: Use adaptive components like NavigationSplitView and SizeClass modifiers. Design for compact width (iPhone) first, then expand for regular width (iPad/Mac). Test at every size class transition and use the adaptation checklist in the skill.

faq.question: What is the maximum number of tabs I can show in a tab bar?
faq.answer: iOS tab bars display a maximum of 5 tabs. Additional items go into a 'More' section. If you need more than 5 top-level sections, consider restructuring with a sidebar or nested navigation hierarchy.

faq.question: How do I support iPad multitasking (Split View, Slide Over)?
faq.answer: Test your app at every split ratio from narrow to wide. Ensure content reflows appropriately and navigation adapts. Use auto-layout and size classes rather than fixed dimensions. Reference: split-views.md and windows.md for multitasking guidance.

faq.question: What are ornaments in visionOS?
faq.answer: Ornaments are visionOS UI elements attached to windows for toolbars and controls without occluding content. They appear at window edges (top, bottom, leading, trailing). Use ornaments for persistent controls while keeping main content visible. Reference: ornaments.md.

faq.question: Should I use SwiftUI or UIKit for navigation components?
faq.answer: SwiftUI provides built-in adaptivity with NavigationSplitView, TabView, and NavigationStack that automatically handle size class changes. UIKit requires more manual work with UISplitViewController and UINavigationController. For new apps, prefer SwiftUI unless you have specific UIKit requirements.
